metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the judge's structured output schema (field names, types, required fields, enums) in the plan — at minimum: `relation`, `shared_artifact` (the cited concrete artifact), `confidence`, and `abstain` flag.
findings.finding: The judge output schema is never defined in the plan. The plan references 'structured output' and a `RunRequest` but provides no field names, types, required/optional status, or validation rules for what the judge returns. An implementer cannot derive the Pydantic model from the plan text.
findings.location: What section — 'A pairwise judge (RunRequest, structured output) over each stage-1 candidate'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC (e.g., `pytest tests/test_stage2_judge.py -k 'related_not_flagged or duplicate_flagged'` for the eval ACs, `jq '.findings | length <= 3' output.json` for the cap AC) so done-ness is deterministic.
findings.finding: No proving command is described for any acceptance criterion — the ACs state outcomes but provide no deterministic check (test invocation, fixture assertion, CLI command) that would produce evidence of pass/fail in-session.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — all five bullet points

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the aggregation rule for conflicting two-ordering verdicts (e.g., 'escalate to abstain if the two orderings disagree on relation type' or 'accept the relation only when both orderings independently agree; otherwise treat as related-but-distinct'), and surface this rule in AC #1.
findings.finding: The aggregation rule for conflicting verdicts across the two orderings is under-specified. When ordering A→B yields 'duplicate' and ordering B→A yields 'related-but-distinct', the plan says only 'both orderings aggregated' without defining the aggregation function (AND-gate, majority/average confidence, escalate-to-abstain, etc.). The hardest cases — where position bias actually fires — are exactly the ones that land in disagreement, so undefined behavior there undermines the mechanism's correctness claim.
findings.location: What section ('both orderings aggregated') and AC #1 ('order-flip does not change the verdict for clear cases')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC #1: 'When the two orderings yield conflicting relation labels, the pair is treated as low-confidence and subject to the abstain rule (not surfaced as a finding), regardless of individual confidence scores.' This is consistent with the existing abstain-on-low-confidence clause and closes the gap without extra complexity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete relation enum (all six: `blocks`, `depends_on`, `relates_to`, `duplicates`, `supersedes`, `discovered_from`) in the judge output schema section, and specify which subset the judge may emit for overlap findings.
findings.finding: The plan names three relation values the judge output schema must emit (`duplicates`, `supersedes`, `depends_on`) but the live `rebar link` CLI accepts exactly six relations (`blocks`, `depends_on`, `relates_to`, `duplicates`, `supersedes`, `discovered_from`). The plan never defines the full enum, leaving `blocks`, `relates_to`, and `discovered_from` unaccounted for. An executor building the judge's output schema cannot know whether these are forbidden, silently mapped, or included.
findings.location: What section — '…the relation (duplicates/supersedes/depends_on)…'; AC line 3 — 'Each finding names … the relation'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Judge output schema' subsection that defines the full Pydantic model, e.g.: `relation: Literal['duplicates', 'supersedes', 'depends_on', 'relates_to']` (or all six), with a note that `blocks` and `discovered_from` are excluded from overlap verdicts and why.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the aggregation decision rule for conflicting orderings (e.g., require agreement in both orderings to surface, or define a confidence-weighted majority rule), co-located with the output schema.
findings.finding: The plan states 'both orderings aggregated (position bias)' as a processing protocol but never specifies the aggregation/decision rule when the two orderings yield conflicting verdicts (e.g., A→B says `duplicates`, B→A says `related-but-distinct`). This tie-breaking rule is referenced but not co-located with any output schema or rubric prompt definition.
findings.location: What section — 'both orderings aggregated (position bias)'; AC line 1 — 'order-flip does not change the verdict for clear cases'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' section: 'When the two orderings disagree, the pair is NOT surfaced (conservative default); it counts as related-but-distinct. Only pairs where both orderings independently yield the same non-related-but-distinct verdict are eligible for surfacing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the rationale for the bounded pairwise LLM judge pattern (both-orderings aggregation, abstain-without-artifact default, advisory-only ~3-cap).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new pipeline stage with novel design choices (bounded pairwise LLM judge, position-bias aggregation, abstain logic, advisory-only output cap), but mentions no ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or design-doc update to capture this architectural decision for future maintainers.
findings.location: Scope section / What section
findings.suggested_fix: Create docs/adr/stage2-pairwise-judge.md covering: (1) why both orderings are required (position-bias), (2) why the default is related-but-distinct without a cited artifact, (3) why findings are advisory-only and capped at ~3, (4) how K bounds cost.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the labeled fixture: its location in the repo, format (e.g., JSONL with fields `{pair_id, ticket_a, ticket_b, ground_truth_relation}`), and minimum size (e.g., ≥10 pairs including at least 3 true-duplicate and 3 related-but-distinct cases).
findings.finding: The 'labeled fixture' referenced in the eval-spec AC is not described — its content, format, location, and minimum size are all absent, creating a fixture-invalidity risk.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — 'K and any confidence threshold are tuned on the labeled fixture'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the artifact-citation gating rule with a specific schema field definition (e.g., `shared_artifact: str | None`) and specify the gate: 'if `shared_artifact` is null or empty, classify as related-but-distinct regardless of `relation`.'
findings.finding: The 'REQUIRED to cite the shared concrete artifact' rule is a gating decision protocol, but it is stated only in prose — not co-located with any schema field definition or validation rule. There is no specification of which output field carries the citation, what constitutes a valid citation value, or how the gating logic reads it.
findings.location: What section — 'REQUIRED to cite the shared concrete artifact (same root cause / same file / same user-visible behavior), DEFAULTS to related-but-distinct without one'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define K explicitly in the plan (e.g., 'K = maximum number of LLM calls per ticket, default 40 for ~20 candidates × 2 orderings') so the executor has an actionable starting value.
findings.finding: K (the cost-bounding parameter) is referenced twice but never defined — an executor cannot implement the bounded judge without knowing what K represents (call count, token budget, candidate limit, etc.).
findings.location: ## What — 'Cost is bounded by K (flat in corpus size)'; ## Acceptance Criteria — 'K and any confidence threshold are tuned on the labeled fixture'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'K is the maximum number of pairwise LLM calls per input ticket (default: 2 × |stage-1 candidates|, i.e. 40 for 20 candidates); the optional cascade reduces this to 2 × |survivors|.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC covering error/degraded paths: (a) LLM API failure → judge abstains or propagates a safe error, (b) malformed/missing structured output → pair is classified related-but-distinct and not surfaced, (c) split-ordering verdict → tie-breaking or abstain rule is defined and tested.
findings.finding: The eval spec (AC5) addresses precision and recall on labeled fixtures but the plan specifies no coverage for failure/error paths in the new judge logic: LLM API timeout or error, malformed/missing structured output from the LLM, or split-verdict cases where the two orderings produce conflicting relation types. For a new behavior that invokes an external LLM boundary, these paths need at least one acceptance criterion each.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 / 'eval spec' section
findings.suggested_fix: Append to Acceptance Criteria: 'AC6: On LLM API error or malformed structured output the pair is treated as related-but-distinct and not surfaced. AC7: When the two orderings produce conflicting relation types, the aggregation rule (e.g. abstain, take the lower-confidence verdict, or default to related-but-distinct) is defined and covered by a labeled fixture case.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define K concretely (e.g. max LLM calls per invocation, or max candidate pairs evaluated), state its unit, and specify a minimum labeled-fixture cardinality so the AC 'K and any confidence threshold are tuned on the labeled fixture' is verifiable.
findings.finding: The plan's core cost-bounding claim ('Cost is bounded by K, flat in corpus size') relies on a variable K that is never defined, quantified, or tied to a concrete labeled fixture size. The AC explicitly requires 'K and any confidence threshold are tuned on the labeled fixture,' yet neither K's value/unit, the fixture's cardinality, nor an estimated LLM-call count (e.g. ~20 candidates → ~760 ordered pairs at some call cost) is stated. This makes the cost-bounding AC unverifiable as written.
findings.location: What section — 'Cost is bounded by K (flat in corpus size)'; Acceptance Criteria — 'K and any confidence threshold are tuned on the labeled fixture'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Cost model' subsection under 'What': 'K = <N> LLM calls per run (both orderings × up to <M> stage-1 candidates = at most 2M calls); the labeled fixture must contain at least <F> pairs (including ≥1 true-duplicate and ≥1 related-but-distinct pair) to tune K and the confidence threshold.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback for an abstained judge verdict: e.g., 'abstained pairs are treated as related-but-distinct and not surfaced; they do not consume an advisory finding slot.'
findings.finding: The plan says the judge 'abstains on low confidence' but never specifies the fallback: what happens to an abstained pair (dropped silently, treated as related-but-distinct, re-queued, surfaced with a flag), and whether an abstain consumes one of the ~3 advisory finding slots.
findings.location: What section — 'abstains on low confidence'; AC line 4 — implicit (never-mutates), no explicit abstain fallback AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' section: 'An abstained pair is treated identically to related-but-distinct: not surfaced, not counted against the ~3 cap. The confidence threshold triggering abstain is a tunable parameter set in the eval fixture (see Eval spec).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the aggregation rule explicitly (e.g., 'agree → use that verdict; disagree → abstain or use lower-confidence verdict') so an executor can implement it deterministically.
findings.finding: The ordering-aggregation rule is unspecified — 'both orderings aggregated' is stated but not how to aggregate (majority vote, average confidence, stricter of two, etc.), leaving a non-trivial design decision to the executor.
findings.location: ## What — 'both orderings aggregated (position bias)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Aggregation rule: if both orderings yield the same verdict, use it; if they disagree, classify as abstain (or related-but-distinct if confidence is below threshold).'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the call-count formula explicitly (e.g. '2 × |stage-1 candidates| LLM calls per query ticket, ≈40 calls for K=20') and provide a rough cost estimate to make the cost bound verifiable.
findings.finding: The cost bound 'bounded by K (flat in corpus size)' is asserted without specifying the actual call count formula. With both orderings, each of the ≈20 stage-1 candidates requires 2 LLM calls, giving 2K calls per query ticket — but the plan does not state whether K is the candidate count (≈20), the pair count, or something else, and does not quantify the optional cascade's savings.
findings.location: ## What — last sentence ('Cost is bounded by K (flat in corpus size)') and optional cascade bullet
findings.suggested_fix: State in ## What: 'Each query ticket incurs 2 × K LLM calls (both orderings), where K ≤ 20 (stage-1 output cap). At ~$X per 1K tokens and ~Y tokens per call, the per-query cost ceiling is $Z. The optional cascade targets reducing K to K\'<K survivors before the strong judge.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify retry/backoff for transient LLM errors, a graceful-degradation path when the LLM is unavailable (e.g., skip stage-2 and surface no advisory findings with a logged warning), and handling for malformed structured-output responses.
findings.finding: The plan adds LLM calls (RunRequest, structured output) as a new external integration point but specifies no error handling, retry/backoff, or graceful-degradation strategy for LLM failures.
findings.location: What section / AC: 'Judges each candidate pair in both orderings'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the design: exponential-backoff retry (e.g., 3 attempts) for transient errors; on persistent failure, log a warning and return an empty advisory list rather than propagating the error; validate structured output schema before consuming it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'clear cases' with a measurable threshold (e.g., 'both orderings must agree when each individually reports confidence ≥ 0.8') so the criterion is deterministically evaluable.
findings.finding: AC: 'order-flip does not change the verdict for clear cases' uses the subjective qualifier 'clear cases', making it impossible to evaluate deterministically without a definition of what constitutes a 'clear case'.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — first bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite the AC to state a specific measurable outcome (e.g., 'Precision ≥ X% and recall ≥ Y% on the labeled fixture, as measured by `python eval_judge.py --fixture fixtures/stage2_pairs.jsonl`') instead of the effort-framed 'are tuned'.
findings.finding: AC: 'K and any confidence threshold are tuned on the labeled fixture' is effort-framed ('tuned') rather than stating a specific observable outcome, making it unmeasurable as written.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — fifth bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align 'Never auto-links' (What) and 'Never mutates links' (AC4) to a single, precise statement — e.g. 'The judge never creates or modifies any link record; all suggested links are emitted as advisory text only.'
findings.finding: 'Never auto-links' (What section) and 'Never mutates links' (AC4) describe subtly different constraints — one forbids automatic link creation, the other forbids mutation of existing links — leaving the actual behavioral invariant ambiguous.
findings.location: What section ('Never auto-links') vs AC line 4 ('Never mutates links')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace both phrases with one canonical statement in both sections: 'The stage-2 judge NEVER writes to the link store; findings are advisory text only.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability requirements: log/metric for LLM call latency and error rate, abstain rate per batch, and finding rate; alert if error rate exceeds threshold.
findings.finding: The plan introduces LLM calls as new failure points but specifies no observability (metrics, logs, or traces) for call latency, failures, or the rate at which pairs are abstained vs. classified.
findings.location: What section / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Emit structured logs per batch: {pairs_evaluated, abstained, findings_emitted, errors, latency_ms}. Expose as a metric endpoint or structured log that can feed an alert.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default for the cascade (e.g., 'disabled by default; if enabled, use embedding cosine similarity ≥ 0.6 as pre-filter') so the executor has a concrete starting point.
findings.finding: The 'optional cascade: cheap pre-filter → strong judge only on survivors' defers the design choice of what algorithm constitutes the pre-filter and under what condition the cascade is enabled, with no stated default.
findings.location: ## What — 'Optional cascade: cheap pre-filter → strong judge only on survivors, to bound cost further.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an initial default confidence threshold and a measurable tuning target (e.g., 'start at 0.7; tune to achieve ≥90% precision and ≥80% recall on the labeled fixture') so the executor can act without clarification.
findings.finding: The confidence threshold for abstention and surfacing is described as something 'tuned on the labeled fixture' with no starting value or range, deferring a real design decision to the executor.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — 'K and any confidence threshold are tuned on the labeled fixture'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or explicitly mark it out-of-scope) for the optional pre-filter cascade — specifying what 'survivors' means and how cost-bounding is validated.
findings.finding: The 'optional cascade' (cheap pre-filter → strong judge on survivors) is described as a deliverable in the 'What' section but has no covering acceptance criterion, leaving it unverifiable.
findings.location: What section (last bullet) and Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Either add an AC such as 'When the optional cascade is enabled, only pairs passing the pre-filter are sent to the strong judge, and total LLM calls ≤ K × filter_pass_rate' or move the cascade explicitly to OUT scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the advisory-findings cap (~3) and confidence threshold are runtime-configurable parameters (e.g., env var or config file), not hardcoded constants.
findings.finding: The ~3 advisory-findings cap and the confidence threshold are stated as constants in the plan ('at most ~3 candidates', tuned on fixture) with no indication they will be configurable after initial tuning, despite being the parameters most likely to need adjustment in production.
findings.location: What section / AC line: 'At most ~3 candidates surface'
findings.suggested_fix: Define STAGE2_MAX_FINDINGS (default 3) and STAGE2_CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD as named config parameters so they can be adjusted without code changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a prior-art section (or a sentence in ## Why) that names at least two established alternatives (e.g. cross-encoder rerankers, existing LLM-as-judge frameworks) and explicitly justifies why the bespoke bounded pairwise LLM judge is preferred over them.
findings.finding: The plan builds a bespoke bounded LLM pairwise judge for ticket deduplication without acknowledging or ruling out established prior art: the LLM-as-judge pattern (Zheng et al. 2023 'Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench'), cross-encoder rerankers (e.g. SentenceTransformers cross-encoders), or semantic-dedup libraries (e.g. LlamaIndex dedup, SemDeDup). The novelty of the specific combination (artifact-citation gate + both-orderings + abstain) is asserted, not justified against these alternatives.
findings.location: ## What / ## Why sections

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the target LLM/API and confirm it supports the structured output schema required (relation enum + cited artifact + confidence), or specify a fallback parsing strategy if constrained decoding is unavailable.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'RunRequest, structured output' as the mechanism for the judge but does not name or verify which LLM/API is used, nor confirm that it supports the required structured-output schema (e.g. JSON mode, function-calling, constrained decoding). This is an unverified capability assertion.
findings.location: ## What — first bullet ('A pairwise judge (RunRequest, structured output)')

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
